comments.title: Review of Transforming Darkness and Tolle's Pain-Body
comments.content:
Good Evening,

If you're in the US, I hope you're making good use of the holiday weekend, but amongst the merriment, I hope you'll join me in raising a toast to those who have given their lives for this country.

To Share: After someone asked about working with Jung's Shadow last week right after I read an excerpt from a book on that very topic, considering it's Jung we're talking about, I figured synchronicity must be afoot, and so I ordered a copy of the book and read it. It's very short, so I was able to collect my thoughts on it here, for anyone interested: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_transforming_darkness

To Ask: I learned of a concept of Eckhart Tolle's called the "pain-body" in the book on shadow work I mentioned in my "to share" above. Apparently, the "pain body" is supposed to be a semi-autonomous part of your subtle body made up of pain, negative feeling, and so forth, and since it is semi-autonomous and needs more negative feeling to "feed," when it gets triggered (stuff that you've repressed or don't identify with comes up), the pain-body seizes control and makes you act in ways that guarantee its supply of negative feeling (picking fights, succumbing to addictions, seeing everything in the worst possible light, et cetera). Davis proposes that the Pain-Body is basically another way of looking at the Shadow, and that Tolle's methods for dealing with it can help with Shadow work.

Two questions on this, if I may, mainly for JMG, but I welcome anyone else's thoughts as well:
1. Does this map onto anything you know about the subtle bodies? If accurate, it sounds like a primarily astral phenomenon, possibly with some effects on the etheric.
2. Does this strike you as having any validity as either an explanatory framework or a method of dealing with repressed/rejected aspects of the self?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review of Transforming Darkness and Tolle's Pain-Body
replies.content:
To the fallen. (Clink.)

Physical pain leaves traces in the etheric body, emotional and mental pain leaves similar traces in the astral body; I suppose you could probably treat the interaction among these traces as a "body" if you wanted to.

2) The only question that matters, to my mind, is whether techniques associated with that approach work. If they do, that's the only justification they need.

replies.title: Re: Review of Transforming Darkness and Tolle's Pain-Body
replies.content:
Interesting! To your questions, Jeff, yes, the concept of the pain body (it's existence, not the 'seizing control' bit) echoes the concept I work with in my form of energy healing, which is mapping up the emotional pain points juxtaposed over the physical body, kind of like an emotional pain body. Certain emotional imbalances tend to show up as physical illnesses, usually very metaphorically… like tied-up emotions about communication tend to have throat problems, intolerance can lead to deafness (don’t want to ‘hear’ others), indecisiveness can lead to teeth problems (‘chewing over’ an idea forever wears them away), etc, etc etc.

In this way the idea of this body becomes a really handy tool for dealing with subconscious aspects of the self, because you sort of meditate on the underlying symbolism of the physical issue and match it up with the same-feeling emotional tendency. I’ve pretty much always found it a really useful tool, in my experience the body really acts like a mirror of the subconscious. Plus, resolving the emotional imbalance habit often heals the physical body side of things once there’s not so much emotional pain there :-).

I disagree with him about it seizing you, in my experience the emotional habit you keep around is because you always get some sort of ‘benefit’ from it (the benefit is not real, of course, and part of healing is replacing filling that need in a more wholesome way.)

My blessings to you also if you will have them, Ekaterina

replies.title: Re: Review of Transforming Darkness and Tolle's Pain-Body
replies.content:
Thank you for this! For what it's worth, the "pain-body" was presented less as a way to deal with physical pain and healing, and more with emotional problems. Davis basically says the pain-body is the Jungian Shadow (or at least a part of it) and so recommends the techniques as a method of processing repressed emotions and their associated fallout. Of course, he also points out in other parts of the book that a lot of physical ailments are caused, at least partially, by unprocessed emotions, as you say.

Also, though I haven't read Tolle except for the excerpts quoted in Davis's book, I don't think he would disagree about the "benefit" from the negative habit, and I'm more certain that Davis wouldn't either. I think his point is that usually the sought is not wholly acknowledged or known by the conscious part of the self, so the sub-conscious part that he calls the pain-body nudges you into doing stuff (something like: some part of you wanted to feel powerful and vent some anger, so it picks a fight, but your conscious self is like "why was that guy being such a jerk? he had it coming!").

Anyhow, thanks again for sharing your experience,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review of Transforming Darkness and Tolle's Pain-Body
replies.content: I forget where, but I was recommended the book "Feeding your Demons" by Tsultrim Allione. It's about a (supposedly, I can't speak for the validity or historicity of the techniques) Tibetan Buddhist practice of Chöd, but simplified for a western audience. Basic concept is embracing your (inner) demons, giving them what they need from you until they reintegrate as part of you and become helpers. I have only played with it, but it was an interesting concept, and had relatively positive results for me in the limited areas I've used it.

comments.content:
I recently stumbled on a weird TV show called Hazbin Hotel. As odd as it might sound, I think it's an important piece of media, because it's a major step forward in the process of people redefining their old views to make a conversion to fundamentalist Christianity easier. Specifically, I think it is a sign that Progressives have begun to redefine Progressivism itself as evil and demonic.

I can't help but note one of the defining features of the show is that it is set in a Hell that it portrays as a thoroughly modern city. It is also, as the show goes to remarkably great lengths to demonstrate, a thoroughly miserable and terrible place to be. Heaven meanwhile is shown to be a much more pleasant place to live: it has its dark side, but overall, seems far better; and it is portrayed as an old fashioned kind of place.

The show also seems determined to try to portray Lucifer in a positive light. This is not new, but it does so in a way I find quite fascinating: it essentially makes him into a failed Progressive. He is an idealistic dreamer who sought to make the world better; but made some mistakes and was condemned to Hell because of them, and led innocent others into it; while the residents of Heaven are ignorant of such basic things as how people get into Heaven, and are stuck in a weird kind of stasis.

And this was made, marketed, and is being heavily pushed by Amazon, and is being lauded by the mainstream media, who seem to be unaware of the subtext of the entire thing.

a) Do you think it's possible that I'm right about the subtext?

b) If this does seem plausible to you, how much further do you think things can go before we see people finally snap and adopt the Christian identity they seem to be so eager to adopt? I can't help but worry about the implications of living in a society governed by people eager to redefine their views as literally demonic, and so as bad as a mass conversion of the elites to Fundamentalist Christianity will be, think the sooner it happens, the better.

Do you have an opinion on Idaho, where I live? It has a well-earned reputation as a conservative state, and there are many ostensibly religious people here, but in the 23 years I've lived here as a non-Christian, I've had no problems. Of the very few who want to talk religion, it's overwhelmingly about THEIR religion. They think I'm kidding when I say I'm a Heathen. And I'm happy to let them think that. To my mind, conservative doesn't inherently mean Christian.

Another feature of Idaho: Oddballs. There is, or was, a go-your-own-way ethos for generations, or so it seems to me. Perhaps the Mormons are part of that. What little religious friction I've seen here seems to be fundamentalist Christians mumbling about Mormons. Mormons are a significant part of the population. I don't know how many fundamentalists there are, but yet another mega-Church is under construction a few miles from where I live, so it could be the balance is slowly shifting.

I've a few altars in my home: Norse, Minerva, and one for our ancestors. We're on the introvert spectrum, so it is rare that anyone comes to call unless they are paid to do so. The plumber got a bit of a shock a couple weeks ago, I think. That said, I don't want to hide these things on the off chance that someone in my home may feel uneasy. Should I be thinking differently about this?

I don't plaster the outside my house or my car with anything "personal". No signs or stickers proclaiming anything. It's not my style, and honestly, these days I think it's asking for unwanted attention.

Once in a while I think it might be a plan to "convert" to my ancestor's Mormon faith, or revert to my Catholicism of long, long ago for a sense of community and cover. To be honest, I'd feel like a hypocrite. If I don't believe, my gut says I do not belong there. It would simply be disrespectful.

My inclination is to just continue to keep a low profile right here in Idaho. Does this seem reasonable to you?

Thanks as always for your insights,
OtterGirl

Deists, Shakers, Swedenborgians, Spiritualists, Christian Scientists, Pantheists, atheists, and so forth, all flourished there.

There's also an ethos up there of minding one's own business. It's very unhospitable ground for any sort of bullying utopianism, whether on the right or on the left. These are parts of New England that were settled mostly by people who absolutely despised the Puritanism of Masssachusetts and Connecticut. And there are still lots and lots of small farms in the rural areas.

So I think our family will go north if TSHTF. Also, my wife has kinfolk up there with roots that go back to the first half of the 1600s, and we've kept in touch with a few of them.

Even now, the numbers are such that gently and consistently working to assimilate the new converts is a challenge. A good challenge, and we are not complaining! But still, mass movements being what they are, we are certainly not immune.

comments.title: Time and reincarnation
comments.content:
Hi JMG, do you think it's possible for a religion or other metaphysical system to steer the direction of practitioners' reincarnated souls, in particular making it more likely for them to be reincarnated as someone who will likely become a practitioner in the next life (i.e. born to parents of the same religion)?

One more question:

Do you think that reincarnated souls are subject to strictly linear time? I.e. that a person could only be reincarnated after their chronological death, and that their soul could not move backwards in time and reincarnate as a person who lived prior to the last incarnation? Authors I've read discussing the subtle planes like Arthur Powell seem to assume that all levels of reality are subject to linear time as people typically perceive it, so I was wondering if you've encountered anything suggesting that time may work differently on those other planes.

Thanks again for these posts.

replies.title: Re: Time and reincarnation
replies.content:
1) No, what determines that is what your karma needs. If your karma is best resolved by doing a few lives in a given religion, then that's what you'll get.

2) I'm probably going to have to put something about this in the FAQ. All I know is that all my past life memories, and the past life memories of those who I've had good reason to think were remembering clearly, are in strict time sequence.

replies.title: Re: Time and reincarnation
replies.content:
All I know is that all my past life memories, and the past life memories of those who I've had good reason to think were remembering clearly, are in strict time sequence.

Ah, but is there any way to know if they were "sorted" into the chronological sequence you now observe "after the fact"? Is that even a coherent question? Another way to phrase this, are insertions possible in your subjective timeline of previous lives?

On the other hand, if lives are not necessarily lived in order, would we expect at least some people to have "past life" memories of the future? Yet this is not seen, or would it be a mechanism behind prophecy?

[different Anonymous]

replies.title: Re: Time and reincarnation
replies.content:
There is indeed a way to know. Karma is a process of cause and effect; if you see a cause in an earlier life consistently reflected by corresponding effects in later life, you've got evidence that the timeline is correct. I have those, quite consistently.

As for "memories of the future," why, I knew people who claimed to have exactly that source of information about the wonderful world we were all going to enter in 2012. That's not the only example, either. Quite consistently, when people claim to have memories of the future and make claims that can be checked in due time, they're wrong. Thus I tend to discount such claims as yet another mechanism by which peop;le project wish fulfillment fantasies of various kinds onto the blank screen of the future.

(Did you know, btw, that Erich von Daniken's first book, published as Chariots of the Gods? in the US, was titled Memories of the Future in its original European edition?)

replies.title: Re: Time and reincarnation
replies.content:
I'm piggybacking off the original poster here because your answer made me think of something and I've been meaning to ask you (but kept forgetting).

An older man I knew (since deceased) told me this story, and I have no reason not to believe him.

As a young boy, he was visiting a house on a lake with his family for a vacation. Several members of his immediate and extended family were sitting around inside the house, when they suddenly all heard what sounded like a low-flying airplane with a faulty engine. They all ran outside to see what was going on - and saw nothing. They continued to hear the struggling engine, then heard a loud crash/splash sound, and then all immediately went silent. They looked out over the lake for any sign of a plane, but saw nothing. Over the course of the next few days, they asked around and checked the local news, but nobody else had heard anything, and there were no reports in the news. They chalked it up to some sort of weird acoustical phenomenon - perhaps a plane had landed elsewhere and there had been some sort of weird echo from the hills/water, or maybe a boat had sounded like a plane, even though they didn't see any boats - and moved on.

About a decade later, the son of one of the women who had been present (the son had not been there) was flying a small plane over the very same lake, as part of a military exercise, and something went wrong with the engine. The plane faltered and crashed into the water, killing him.

Is there an explanation for this phenomenon? Could the whole family have had a collective premonition of the young man's death in some years' time? Was there some sort of "echo in time" of the sound of the crash that reached them in the "past"? Or is the mundane explanation (it really was just a plane landing behind hills or a boat somewhere) more likely?

As noted, I have no reason to disbelieve the person who told me this, and he assures me that everyone else heard it too, and that after the accident, they almost all believed that they had "heard" the future plane crash that killed their loved one.

But I am curious to hear what you think of this story.

comments.title: Psychic ability and Magic
comments.content:
Hi Jmg, Some time ago I recall you saying something to the effect that if a person has natural psychic abilities then Magic isn't a good path for them and they're better off not learning formal Magic. I don't recall your exact words but that was the gist of it I think. This resonated with me because I had attempted learning/performing formal Magic a few times and it just felt all wrong, and I do have a decent amount of natural psychic ability. I feel/hear/see (it's difficult to describe) a lot of information that it took me a long time to realize other people don't pick up on. Included in this is sensing the movement of energy and feeling, or not feeling, future events. However, it wasn't until you made that comment about psychics and magic that I realized WHY I had such a problem with formal magical training. It was like trying "learn" how to do something that I already could do but in a completely different and more convoluted way.

Back in about 2011 I joined the AODA and started working through The Druidry Handbook. This was when I ended up deciding that formal ritual etc wasn't right for me. I still talk to trees, other flora and fauna, and other nature spirits. I also make offerings, do my own protection rituals, and similar things. I think the main difference is I follow what feels right to me rather than a formal pre-written ritual. I also practice a couple forms of divination, though at least half the time the messages come from my psychic sensing or reading of signs and omens.

I recently checked in with the AODA and it turns out my membership is for life. My question is, is it appropriate for me to call myself a Druid despite the fact that I don't practice formal magic?

Thanks,

FSH

replies.title: Re: Psychic ability and Magic
replies.content:
1) The important thing to keep in mind is that magic is only one subset of occultism; magic is to occultism what chemistry, say, or biology is to science as a whole. You can be a scientist without being a biologist -- ask any physicist, astronomer, or geologist, for starters! -- and in the same way, you can be an occultist without practicing magic. People who have strong psychic gifts are generally better off not practicing magic, since in many cases formal magical ritual doesn't play well with psychism.

2) Magic isn't that important in Druidry; in fact, it's fair to say that most Druids don't practice magic. Thus you can certainly use the term for yourself.

I was already vaguely aware that occultism consisted of more than just magical practice (and the theories/philosophies that go with that). comments.title: Wish to discharge familial karma
comments.content:
There are undesirable patterns in my family relationships that I think must come from past life involvements. One family member has a very aggravating habit that I find *incredibly* annoying. It’s persisted for 50 years, so I don’t think it will ever stop. It’s a constant daily torment. Another family member has psychological issues from the deep past that I’d be a lot happier not having to deal with.

It’s not all bad, I don’t think any of us is a hardcore narcissist or sociopath, but I really, *really* don’t want these patterns to follow me from lifetime to lifetime as I suspect they have probably done up to now.

Is there anything I can do to free myself from this karmic burden a little faster? I really dread the thought of having to recapitulate this again and again.

My magic background is in Golden Dawn, Hellenic style, with no ritual more complex than the Rose Cross. I also invoke the violet flame fairly regularly, though I am pretty new at this.

BTW, I can’t just walk out on these people. They are heavily dependent on me, I am the de facto caretaker and abandonment by me would lead to very bad practical consequences for them. I’d like to be able to arrange for help with my responsibilities, and hope to do so in future, but right now it’s not practical. I am pretty much on my own.

replies.title: Re: Wish to discharge familial karma
replies.content:
You're dealing with the karma in the classic way right now: accepting it, dealing with it, and not trying to run away from it. The violet flame invocation's a useful tool here, btw, as it can help clear away karma -- you might consider finding or creating a decree that assists in that.

That's the easy part of the answer. What follows is the hard part.

Carl Jung used to point out that whatever irritates us in others is always a reflection of ourselves. That's the projection of the shadow: the things that annoy us in others are our own bad habits that we can't bear to notice. I'm going to suggest that you spend some time every day doing journaling about the irritating habit you mention, with the intention of seeing how it reflects your own attitudes or behaviors back to you. Realize that, and first of all, the habit will stop bothering you -- and second, you'll release the karma that keeps that interaction stuck in place.

replies.title: Re: Wish to discharge familial karma
replies.content:
Thanks. I will look into an appropriate decree, as you suggest.

The second part is indeed the hard part. It’s just about impossible for me to imagine not being aggravated as I’ve described. But journaling doesn’t sound prohibitively difficult. Maybe through it I can sort of crab walk toward this otherwise unimaginable objective.

*******

Incidentally, I feel I should let you know that your counsel from last week seems to have helped. I made the offering to Athena - just the daily libation of olive oil - and from an unexpected source I received some advice that I believe may well help with my gnarly housing situation. My state of mind is considerably improved.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG! Once more, thanks a lot for answering our questions.

Today I'd like to know your opinion on the plethora of, shall we call them, "neo-gnostic" movements within occultism, mostly of a dark and "left hand path" bent.

They seem to believe in a demiurge or demiurgical deity/condition they seek to escape from, but instead of "returning to the pleroma" like regular primitive christians later deemed "gnostic," they seek to "escape the cosmos," diving into chaos and becoming deities, getting "deified" like they say. Is anything like this even remotely possible? I find the concept quite abhorrent and juvenile, but a lot of them deal in very dark modes of sorcery, like Quimbanda or Palo Mayombe, not to mention the nietszchian undertones they graft into everything. They seem to mean business and get "somewhere."

I wonder if all they really manage to do is go hopping at the edges of the cosmos among the discarded shells of previously formed individualities, calling them "gods" and managing the stay there in much the same condition, much like Dion Fortune describes in her Cosmic Doctrine if I understood that part correctly. If this is so, am I right to assume they will eventually snap out of it and continue with the "proper path" of evolution? Also, if one manages to achieve such a state, am I right to intepret that state as just "pausing" their evolution and remaining as just plain "human level" conscious beings living outside the "regular cosmos" (or at the borders of it)?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think Dion Fortune's analysis is a good one here. These people fling themselves to the outer edge of the cosmos in an attempt to break through the Ring-Pass-Not into the Penumbra, the "heap of broken images" left behind by the dead ends and might-have-beens of Cosmic evolution. Some of them will make it, though the great majority won't. Those that do will add to the collection of dead ends and might-have-beens, and stay there until the Cosmic cycle swings over from day to night and what Fortune describes as "the waters of the Cosmic tide" flows over them. Then whatever's left of them -- for they'll have been unraveling themselves bit by bit the whole time, as they react against the order that maintains them in existence -- will be dissolved down to basic components, and those will be recycled in other forms by other beings.

The majority, those who can't get through the Ring-Pass-Not, will stay stuck on the outer edge of existence until it finally sinks in that they're wasting their time and could go do something less dull. It may take a while.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Pardon me if I am too dull, but...

your description of the Penumbra sounds remarkably like my concept of the Christian Hell. Not max-security prison with the lava lakes and sadomasochistic, horned beings... but the pigheaded (and self defeating) rebellion against the Universal order that supports their very existence.

Furthermore, in Fortean though, the deepest layers of Purgatory are indistinguishable from Hell proper from the subjective point of view. What these non-damned still have is the possibility of think things through and change their opinions, which should lead to their eventual redemption, though on a unimaginable time-frame.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The great difference as I see it lies in how easy it is to get there. To listen to most people who belong to your religion, it's very easy indeed. From my perspective, it's almost superhumanly difficult -- you have to develop all the capacities that would take you above the human level, and then use them in a deliberately perverse manner. A Catholic equivalent would be to suggest that the only people who end up in hell are failed saints.

That difference accepted, sure, there's a lot of similarity. Fortune was a Christian, after all; furthermore, I don't see any reason to think that Christian visionaries and mystics are wrong by definition, just biased by their beliefs -- as, of course, are the rest of us.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I like the term failed saints!!! And it traces nicely to the image of Lucifer; he was the equivalent of High Priest in angelic society after all. St. Michael Archangel, on the other hand, was a mister nobody from the lower classes. He stood out only because he had the stubborn courage to say the other one in his face: "It ain't right. Who (may stand up to the stature of) like God!!!"

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've heard that claim about Michael from some people; I'm not at all sure where it comes from, as I don't recall ever encountering it in angel lore until quite recently. Do you know the source of it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I first read it in Ft. Antonio Fortea's "Historia del Mundo Angélico" (subtitled: chronicle of the creation of Thrones and Powers, narration of the test and fall of Seraphim and Querubs). In the prologue, Fortea makes claims his sources are the Bible, Thomas Aquinas, Aristotle and the writings of the 17th century Spanish abbess and mystic Sor María de Jesus Ágreda.

This last source was heavily credited with the part of the book that deals with the "test" that generation of angelic souls had to pass (or not) in order to achieve their current form. Since the Lucifer vs Michael story lays at the core of the issue, I guess she might well be the first to make that claim. As for why has it reached you in recent times, I don't know... a recent translation of her works to English, maybe?

[update] though, on second thoughts... I dimly recall having heard some similar narrative in Catechism class, sometime around ages 8 or 9. Maybe this have been around in the Spanish speaking world for some time and it is just now percolating to the Anglosphere.

comments.title: Protections while re-watching a Horror Movie; Healing
comments.content:
Dear JMG, healers and readers of MM,

I am asking a question (and in 3 asking for healing energies) about what protections I might want to (and be able to) put in place while re-watching a horror fairy-tale.

Background story:
Last night, I was thinking about some of my weird sensitivities, particularly that all childhood I was scared or at least disturbed by TV noises (and my parents would watch TV in the next room every evening). It was not the first time I was pondering this, but an unexpected answer came to me in the form of a very old memory: as a very young child, not yet able to speak (about 1 year old?) I accidentally watched a horror – my mum sat me in front of TV, read the title “fairy-tale” in TV program and went cooking in the next room, occasionally checking on me. As this very early memory emerged, it came with intense fear, and as I checked – and found the information about the horror online (it was "The Sorcerer's Apprentice, animated horror fairy-tale from 1978, directed by Zeman"), more feelings and physical pain in my throat and upper chest appeared and only slowly have been going away; I also had a bad dream, bordering on nightmare last night.

I do not remember much else except that as a young child I experienced this horror also bodily – I could not move with the fear.

Now, I would like to resolve this:
1) Do you think it is a good idea to watch, process the fears and perhaps do the LBRP whenever I might feel overwhelmed by them? (I am intensely emotionally involved, so divination would probably not work.) Or would another approach be better? (But I cannot think of any.)
2) Can you think of any other simple protections I could put in place? (I intend to pray to two Deities – I know, this probably sounds overblown and silly, but the pain yesterday was pretty intense and is lingering…)
3) I would also like to ask healers willing to send me healing energies to send them, as my throat and upper chest continue to hurt, and my hands are still quite shaky, even now.

Thank you.

With regards,
Markéta

replies.title: Re: Protections while re-watching a Horror Movie; Healing

3) Positive energies incoming.

This kind of thing can be a major issue. Sara spent years having nightmares about hideous blobby things, and only in her forties finally remembered that they were all based on a horror movie her brother had on the television when he was babysitting her, back when she was three or four.

On 1) a typical part of "exposure therapy" for strong fears in psychological practice is to start very small and gradually increase intensity, so that each step feels a bit challenging, but not overwhelming. As JMG said, journaling might be one step in the process. Another might be viewing still images from the film. Another might be watching part of it on mute (or just listening without the picture, depending on what feels easier/harder). The point is, you don't have to "jump right in the deep end" if that sounds daunting, and it might not be helpful, even if you made yourself.

3) Healing energy incoming!

comments.title: Occult & astrological history
comments.content:
(my query is in the last paragraph)

I've always been into history, but until fairly recently, I was limited by rationalist history, which peers back dimly to the Bronze Age, and not far beyond it. Now, I've learned of civilizational cycles: Polarian, Hyperborean, Lemurian, Atlantean, our own fifth cycle, and two more yet to come. I'd come across legends and whispers of course, especially in mythology, and fictional worlds like Middle Earth or Conan's Hyborian Age. I never entirely ruled out such tales, but now I have a far better grasp of the grand scheme of things.

I've also become familiar with astrological ages, such as the preceding Piscean Age, represented by major world religions and especially by Christianity, and the Age of Aquarius which we're entering. Despite the hippy-dippy-trippy interpretations of Aquarius, we won't all be enlightened, singing kumbaya surrounded by oceans of lemonade (to reference one of your blog posts from awhile back); instead, flawed human life will go on, just under a different set of principles, new virtues and vices.

I also noticed that our current civilizational cycle seems to have begun with either Leo or Virgo, when Atlantis fell; in which case we'd be about halfway through the dozen signs. Then there's the signs themselves: I gather that Leo melted the glaciers, Cancer was a time of growth, Gemini brought trade and cultural exchange, Taurus was a time of stability/construction/bull worship, Aries brought imperialism, and the recent Pisces was a time of spirituality and paradox (oversimplifying here).

So my questions are: do the 7 civilizational cycles each last for 12 signs/ages? And what will be the character of the Age of Aquarius? I'm thinking it'll be a time of innovation, of destroy & rebuild, as industrial-technological civilization crashes, and we find new ways forward, piecing together modern tech in a far more sustainable manner, but that's just my speculation. Also, I think the astrological ages would be worthy of a blog post, in due time after other priorities of course.

replies.title: Re: Occult & astrological history
replies.content:
1) Whether each cycle lasts a full twelve signs isn't clear from the evidence. I don't think so, since Sundaland (the core region of "Lemuria") sank much less than 25,920 years before the date Plato gives for the fall of Atlantis -- if I recall correctly, Sundaland flooded in stages around 14,000 BC -- but I don't see any reason to be dogmatic about that.

2) As the age of Pisces, ruled by Neptune the planet of mysticism and unity, was an age of mass movements dominated by religion, so the age of Aquarius, ruled by Uranus the planet of invention and individuality, will be an age of eccentric individuals going their own way to triumph or destruction. It'll be a Promethean age, which means that a great deal of it will be spent the way Prometheus ended up in the myth, chained to a mountain with an eagle chewing on his liver. It will be an age of great discoveries and great disasters, in which the one thing nobody will be able to do is to get everyone else to listen up and pay attention. Only when we reach the age of Capricorn will people finally be tired of all the confusion and bizarrerie, and buckle down and get to work practically and collectively to make the world a better place. (And of course that will fail too, in its own way.)

3) I'll consider it.

replies.title: Re: Occult & astrological history
replies.content:
I see, thanks!

Again, I'm struck by how apt astrology can be -- "mysticism and unity" perfectly expresses the past two millennia of religion.
My impression is that the future would be a time of preservation and archival, tinkering and redundancy, working with modern tech to make it more efficient, adaptable, and reliable for what lies ahead. However, maybe that's mostly during the cusp period, and the rise of mad scientists will happen later, once Aquarius is properly underway in a few centuries' time. In any case, we will not be the care bears on shrooms, as pop culture suggests.

As a follow-up, is it possible for a civilizational cycle to exceed the zodiac? Or is its life-span limited to the 12, with possible causes of death happening earlier? (I imagine the Lemurian deviation, building a bridge to demons, was fairly destabilizing, and the Creation myths seem to support this).

As for a blog-post, it would be cool to have a summary of the dominant influences on each age, with examples where feasible, and a general overview of relevant issues like the ones I've discussed (just offering my ideas).

replies.title: Re: Occult & astrological history
replies.content: I have no idea if a cycle of civilizations can go on for more than 25,950 years. It seems unlikely, human beings being what they are, but if it does happen I'd expect that to take place in the seventh and last cycle, which is symbolically ruled by Saturn and will mark the old age of our species.

comments.title: Personalities of divination systems
comments.content:
As a relative newcomer to occultism, divination is one of the things I find most fascinating and enjoyable. That doesn't mean I'm especially good at it, but hopefully time will help there. In one of your answers here last week, you talked about how geomancy tends to give very specific answers. I've also seen ogham described as having a tendency to shift the answer towards spiritual matters. (Unfortunately I can't remember if that was you, Jeff Russel or Kimberly Steele.)

So my question is: if different divination systems have different "personalities", or tendencies, or whatever you prefer to call it, could you briefly sum up how some of the systems you've worked with differ in that respect? I'd also be happy to hear any experiences from the wider commentariat here.

As always, thank you for answering our questions.

replies.title: Re: Personalities of divination systems
replies.content:
It wasn't me who said that about Ogham, certainly. I'd characterize the oracles I've used regularly like this:

Geomancy: very down-to-earth and practical, but don't expect it to take your feelings into account. Not very good for spiritual questions.

Tarot: talkative, almost a chatterbox, and sometimes evasive. The fewer the cards you draw, the more likely you are to get a straight answer.

Ogham: friendly, with a tendency to understate things. Tends to be swayed by your emotional state.

Coelbren: very good for practical questions and interpersonal matters. In my experience, better than any other oracle for career questions. If you've misunderstood the situation, though, it's easy to misread.

Horary astrology: precise but detached, as you'd expect from an oracle that works with outer space. Not so much evasive as excessively subtle.

Gypsy Witch oracle (this is the one that features in my tentacle novels): pleasant and practical. Very much like sitting down with an elderly woman who's just taken the bread out of the oven, and offers you good advice over tea.

I Ching: austere, dignified, mildly amused at best and at worst cold and silent. Treat it with great respect and you'll get better results.

replies.title: Re: Personalities of divination systems
replies.content:
This is exactly the kind of thing I was hoping for. Much appreciated. (And the Geomancy/down to earth pun made me chuckle)

In case anyone else wants to chime in, hearing about the temperament of the runes would be interesting too. While I've spent some time studying them in a historical and academic sense, I know much less about the divinatory side of them.

replies.title: Re: Personalities of divination systems
replies.content: Most people who can do runes can't do Ogham, and most people who can do Ogham can't do runes. It's not true universally, but it's true for me, so I'll have to wait for one of our heathens here to speak up.

replies.title: Re: Personalities of divination systems
replies.content:
Well, since I was both name-checked and the Runes were brought up, I suppose I'm obligated to respond at this point :)

I was certainly one of the people who said that my experience of Ogham had been weighted toward the spiritual, though I don't think I proposed it first - that might have been taylorrose, but I'm also not sure.

Ogham: At any rate, reading JMG's take on the Ogham, it occurs to me that it's possible that I have interpreted the results in a more spiritual manner because that's where a lot of my emotional energy has been the last few years as I've been learning this occultism stuff, including Ogham.

Runes: For me, the Runes seem to put a lot of emphasis on you and what you can do about/learn from/be affected by the situation. As such, it can be useful for certain practical questions, especially about career and self-development. An important aspect of this that I've noticed is a kind of "pessimistic optimism" - the Runes won't hide the bad stuff that's coming, but there's usually a flavor of "but here's what you can take from that to be better/wiser/stronger." All of this is assuming you have a way of getting "murk" staves - reversed, basically. Without murk staves, I find the Runes a little too optimistic, but others don't have that experience.

The Runes also feel appropriate for specifically "heathen" questions - when I'm trying to answer questions about prayers, worship, or the like specifically for heathen Gods, the Runes seem like the best choice.

Hope this helps, and happy to answer any follow up questions if I can,
Jeff

replies.content: I am absolutely terrible at Coelbren divination, to the point of it seeming like it's going out of its way to give me nonsense, even after putting a very long time into its study. I've put that down to having no Welsh ancestry whatsoever, only English. I've had great success with Elder Futhark Runes and Tarot, though. Does this sort of thing happen often?

replies.content: Yes, very much so. Some people are really good at some oracles and can't get anything out of others. There are tarot decks that won't talk to me at all, for example -- and of course the runes -- while Ogham and Coelbren are both practically eager to chat.

replies.title: Re: Personalities of divination systems

Thanks much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Personalities of divination systems
replies.content:
If I may, I‘ll add my two cents about JMG‘s Sacred Geometry Oracle:

In my experience, it‘s great at showing the underlying relations and structures, especially in complex situations. If you just have a quick yes/no question, you‘re probably better off with another oracle, as the Sacred Geometry Oracle will tend to go into details. On occasion, it can be quite blunt. comments.title: Equilibrium and divination questions
comments.content:
Good morning all,

I have been practicing a daily meditation and tarot reading for several months now, and have finally started to be able to see what the cards are telling me, but along with that understanding I have been feeling a sense of disequilibrium in regards to magic the last few weeks as I have gotten a number of very accurate divinations. It has been a feeling of a sort of cognitive dissonance I guess. On some conscious level I accept that materialism is false and that there are layers of reality that I have been ignoring/denying, but also I haven't fully accepted it on some gut level and it is creating a very uncomfortable sensation of being unbalanced. It's like I'm in Plato's cave and have accepted that the shadow of materialism on the wall is just a shadow and not reality, but I am unable or unwilling to step out of the cave at the moment. Is this the main danger of magic that I have seen written about, unbalancing ones internal state? Does anyone who went through giving up disenchantment have any themes for meditation that can help bridge this gap?

As a corollary to the first bit, I have concluded that the time has come to add ritual practice into my routine to try and regain balance, and I had picked up a copy of Celtic Golden Dawn so that i would have access to instructions on the LBRP, but I have been enjoying the other practices since I started reading it a week or so ago so I feel like I should just continue with the book as written. I know CGD uses geomancy though, so my question here is whether it is advisable/possible to practice two types of divination at once? I don't have any problem with learning a new form of divination, but I also am disinclined to give up tarot right when I'm starting to understand it, like switching to trumpet right when I'm starting to play piano with both hands. Would doing two divinations interfere with each other, to where it is advisable to shelve tarot while I am folling CGD and come back to it after?

Thanks,
Sub

replies.title: Re: Equilibrium and divination questions

comments.title: Magical Dating Advice
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

There is a girl in my college who used to show me a lot of interest. I didn't make a move to her at the right time, I was too shy. Now she is acting cold towards me, after I reciprocated her interest too lately. Maybe I came across as too needy or insincere. I really like this girl, but I messed it up inadvertently. As far as I know, she is not dating anyone right now and her school workload is very heavy this term. From a magical point of view, what would you recommend me to do to regain her attraction?

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content:
Never, never, never do magic to try to attract the affection of a specific person. This is one of the two classic beginner's mistakes in magic, and it always ends the same way. If she isn't interested, and you do a working to attract her, she'll go through half an hour or so of feeling unexpectedly warm and affectionate toward you -- probably when you're not around to benefit from it -- and then shake herself and think, "What the hell was I thinking?" and slam back the other way.

Then the spell, having failed, will rebound on you. So you'll end up hopelessly besotted by her, and she won't be able to stand you. Talk about a miserable way to spend a couple of years! I've watched it happen more times than I want to think about, and most of the people I know who teach magic have their own wealth of stories along the same lines.

My advice instead is to stop being affectionate toward her and be friendly but a little distant. If there's any attraction there, she'll proceed to reach out to you.

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: Not OP. What is the second classic beginner's mistake in magic?

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: Trying to get wealth you haven't earned. If you do that, the blowback will cause you to lose wealth or other things you value, at least equal to anything you gain and often much more so.

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content:
And presumably the third is to get involved in a land war in Asia.

(I realize The Princess Bride might have come after you stopped doing movies, but the joke seemed worth the risk)

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: I would certainly advise against using magic to get involved in a land war in Asia! ;-)

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: Thank you for the cautionary advice. What about using magic to improve my own features that I perceive as unattractive?

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: You can't use magic to change your physical body; that's Hollywood Harry Potter crap, not real magic. What you can do is change your attitudes and opinions about yourself, which have far more of an impact on relationships than your physical features. A confident, friendly, lively guy who's a bit homely will have much better chances getting a date than a gorgeous guy who radiates "I'm a loser" from every pore. One option I'd recommend for cleaning out your mental attics and basements generally is the Order of Spiritual Alchemy work, which you can access free of charge here.

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content:
"My advice instead is to stop being affectionate toward her and be friendly but a little distant. If there's any attraction there, she'll proceed to reach out to you."

This reminds me of some basic Taoist principles like effortless action (wu wei) and letting the nature take its course (ziran). It's a pleasant surprise that the Old Master's teachings can be applied to dating as well :)

replies.title: Re: Magical Dating Advice
replies.content: It's also a piece of old, old dating advice. Women aren't attracted to men who are too eager to please them. Very often they're attracted to men who are cool and even a little disdainful to them, because they see such men as a challenge. It's a problem male celibates have had to deal with down through the centuries -- if you genuinely ignore women's sexual charms, tolerably often they start chasing after you.

comments.title: Aquarian Age
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

It's my understanding that we will be fully in the Age of Aquarius in the year 2117. Of course we hear much talk about the "New/Golden Age" with its dawning. Assuming we (humanity) have not gone extinct or blown ourselves up by then, are there any hallmarks you expect Aquarius to usher in, such as some of the qualities associated with its sign (e.g. boom in scientific discovery, emphasis on group work/collaboration and outer (vs inner) work, new paradigms)?

Also, any suggestions to help prepare for one's second Saturn return, or expectations surrounding it?

Many Thanks.

Anthony from Pittsburgh PA

replies.title: Re: Aquarian Age
replies.content:
1) All that prattle about the New Age or the Golden Age is a matter of an overblown sense of entitlement. If we were worthy of a Golden Age we'd be in one. The Aquarian age is ruled, in traditional astrology, by the malefic planet Saturn, and in modern astrology by the equally malefic planet Uranus; that means that we're in for a rough time.

I accept the Uranus attribution, so I expect the Aquarian age to have the strengths and weaknesses of Uranus. He is the planet of the individual and of liberty, but also of eccentricity, derangement, sudden change, and disaster. Expect 2,160 years of great discoveries and great catastrophes. The mass movements based on the emotional acceptance of beliefs that featured so strongly in the Piscean era will give way to a vast number of eccentric individuals and fringe groups each going their own way, as people get tired of the Piscean model of "believin' what you know ain't so" -- or else! One result is that getting people to organize for any purpose at all will become increasingly difficult.

More generally -- well, if you know people whose sun sign is Aquarius, you know that when they're not at their best they tend to be muddled airheads full of impractical notions. Imagine 2,160 years of muddled airheadedness and impractical notions! In some ways that will be a relief after 2,160 years of "I'm only sadistically torturing you for your own good," the downside of Pisces, but by the time the age of Capricorn comes around people will be desperate to actually do something -- and the next stage of the cycle will dawn.

2) I went through mine a few years ago and no preparation did me any good; as far as I know, it never does. All you can do is deal with it as it happens.

"NaCl has a cubic unit cell. It is best thought of as a face-centered cubic array of anions with an interpenetrating fcc cation lattice (or vice-versa). The cell looks the same whether you start with anions or cations on the corners. Each ion is 6-coordinate and has a local octahedral geometry."

comments.title: Random story from the other side of the pond
comments.content:
Thank you JMG for the MMs!

I’m not sure if there’s a question here but somehow I feel I need to tell you about this

I live in the UK and use the train to commute to work. The other day while waiting for the train there was an announcement, there had been a suicide and the train on that platform (where I was waiting) would not be able to go anywhere as the whole section towards that city would be closed with ambulance and police work for a few hours. The announcement clearly stated that people would need to take an alternative train, gave platform, departure time, etc.

This was not my train, I was waiting for the next train to arrive on that same platform, so I understood that I'd have to go to another platform instead.

Now what I found extremely disturbing was that nobody seemed to be able to listen, understand and follow instructions. People remained in the train sitting down even after the message was repeated at least 4 – 5 times within 15 minutes. Some people queued up to ask the train reps exactly the same question and listen to the same answer…and I’m telling you, everyone could speak English, so it wasn’t about lost tourists or anything.

And as I was looking at this my initial amusement became a bit of a nightmare…I started to get stressed and anxious because it felt like I was living in a separate world or reality to theirs…In a way, I felt like a ghost…I could almost see myself saying look you need to listen and go to the other platform…but they really couldn’t hear me, they didn’t hear the overhead message, they didn’t hear the person in front of them asking the same question they would ask again 5 seconds later…I felt like I was in another planet or as if there was an invisible boundary between me and the rest of the world

What was that all about? Any ideas?

replies.title: Re: Random story from the other side of the pond
replies.content: I wish I did. I've noticed that same weird mindlessness tolerably often in recent years -- a complete inability to process events that stray outside the expected narrative. US politics, domestic and foreign, is dominated by it right now; so, of course, was the industrial world's response to the Covid business, and -- well, I could go on at quite some length. It feels to me as though something has gone horribly wrong and a lot of these people, sometime soon, will go marching mindlessly into the sea or something of the same kind.

replies.content: "... I thought I had heard the reverberations of a disquieting wail as my companions vanished; but my power to linger was slight. As if beckoned by those who had gone before, I half-floated between the titanic snowdrifts, quivering and afraid, into the sightless vortex of the unimaginable."

replies.title: Re: More re. 5 Rites
replies.content:
This is of course a complex question, but there are places to look for ideas on the subject. One is Dion Fortune -- her and have a cluster of observations about female rejuvenation. Following this up with her , and , will give some additional pointers.

[Many people are quite dissatisfied with her ideas on the subject of sexuality and so on (obsolete!), but one of the complications of the issue not only that "the past is another country", but so is the present. Fortune worked in the early days of psychoanalytic psychotherapy in Britain. The Medico-Psychological Clinic, where she worked, was aimed at bringing the benefits of psychoanalytic treatment to people who wouldn't normally have access to it -- lower middle class and working class people. The work she did was "psycho-educational", rather than classically analytic, so it was necessary to speak to people where they were at, to their concerns and values, which were generally not those of the classes that usually were interested in, and had access to, formal psychoanalysis. So she had to speak in terms of the values and anxieties of her audience. This was especially true since she was addressing women, young and middle aged, servants and secretaries and shop-girls.]

One of the core ideas is that male desire, and energy, wakes up to find itself focused on its object, while female desire and energy has to do with waking up to being the focus of desire. (She also remarks on "modesty" as being the reflexive response to desire for which one is not ready, or which one does not welcome.) So when she describes the effects of occult training on older women, she describes it in terms of gradually becoming aware of once again being an object of attention, as the rejuvenating effects progress. (In contrast to the more common arc of gradually realizing that one has become invisible as one ages.) This has a lot to do with her ideas about counterchanging polarities of subtle bodies, which our host has described several times.

As for reversing menopause, it might be of interest that some forms of Taoist practice for women include breast massage designed to "reverse the flow of energy", and that are said to cause amenorrhea. (This is said to be reversible when the exercises are no longer practiced.) The downward and outward flow of female (menstrual) energy is analogized to the downward and outward flow of male (seminal) energy.

For older European ideas, it might be worth looking at Hildegard of Bingen, and her idea of "viriditas", which on one level was the lush springtime greenness of human vitality, male or female.

In all the well-developed meditative, mystical, magical traditions, eastern as well as western, there is a strong overlap with medical lore, which includes astrological lore. While the surface teachings are heavily shaped by culture (a celibate monk or nun is not living in the same context as an emperor), the principles uncovered below the surface are often quite similar.

For us, in general, living in the ruins of multiple cultures, sorting this out is like some great work of archaeology, in which we sort through layer after layer of material, finally figuring it out, and then running off proudly to report to someone who looks at us with lifted eyebrows and says, "Didn't you know that already?"

But it's the journey that counts.

LeGrand

comments.title: Fear
comments.content:
I've been listening to the Dune audiobook and was struck by the Litany Against Fear.

“I must not fear. Fear is the mind-killer. Fear is the little-death that brings total obliteration. I will face my fear. I will permit it to pass over me and through me. And when it has gone past I will turn the inner eye to see its path. Where the fear has gone there will be nothing. Only I will remain.”

According to the principles of affirmation this one violates the rule of opposition, and I was wondering if there was something similar with deeper tracks in space that aspiring wizards could use in stressful times.

Thanks

KVD

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: I don't know of one. The odd thing is that, probably because it's been read so many times, the Litany Against Fear works tolerably well even though it breaks the rules.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: In an odd way it works because it acknowledges fear, accepts that you will fear, but the key is that it stipulates how that fear will be dealt with. So removing the 'not' on the first sentence does not really change it as all the rest is positive.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
I see a little of William Walker Atkinson's work here and there in Frank Herbert. For Herbert's Litany Against Fear, compare Atkinson's comment, "all the charms in the world—all the witchcraft and Voodoo material agencies—can produce no other effect than is allowed them by the minds of the persons sought to be affected. Fear and belief determines the degree of receptivity to such influences" (p. 335 in Mind Power).

In general, Herbert is a subtle and deep thinker, who seems to have read a lot of out-of-the-way books.

FWIW, his "Bene Gesserit" is Latin, a hope or propecy which translates "She will have given birth well."

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: Hmm! That makes a great deal of sense -- not least because Atkinson wrote so much on mind and memory training. I'll have to reread Dune one of these days and see how much of the Mentat system is Atkinsonian in origin.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
Far be it from me to step Herbert as a writer, but a quick attempt at changing as little as possible to be consistent with the principles of affirmation (no negation, all present tense. Arguably, you might want to leave off the first two sentences, since they describe fear, rather than your reaction to it), though obviously this version would have no tracks in space, alas:

"Fear is the mind-killer. Fear is the little-death that brings total obliteration. I face my fear. I permit it to pass over me and through me. And when it passes, I turn the inner eye to see its path. Where the fear goes, only I remain."

A bit clumsy, lacking the rhythm of the original.

For what it's worth, as a data point to support our host's answer, I wrote up the litany from the book on a little card my wife stuck on her mirror while she was studying for the bar exam, and she said it helped her deal with her anxiety. Of course, Dune also had some shared significance for us, so that might have contributed.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: The one emendation I consistently make -- and it started as a misremembering! -- is to say the opening bit "I shall not fear" rather than "I must not fear". To my ear it makes the whole thing more of an act of will. Make of that what you will.
